next_steps: Test for BRAF V600E mutation in bone biopsy specimen
Full-body skeletal survey/MRI to map extent of osteosclerotic/lytic lesions
Cardiac MRI and echocardiography to evaluate for periaortic or cardiac involvement (typical of Erdheim-Chester disease)
Immunohistochemistry for CD68 and CD1a to confirm histiocytic/macrophage origin of lesions
Evaluate for systemic organ involvement (e.g., retroperitoneal fibrosis, kidney masses)
Consider referral to a multidisciplinary center specialized in histiocytic disorders

differential_diagnoses: Mixed histiocytic and IgG4-related inflammatory condition
next_steps: Perform immunohistochemistry for CD68 (histiocytic marker) and CD1a (Langerhans marker) on the most recent tibial biopsy specimen.
Test for BRAF V600E mutation and other MAPK pathway mutations (e.g., MAP2K1) in the biopsy specimen.
Order serum IgG4 levels and serum protein electrophoresis with immunofixation.
Obtain cardiac MRI to screen for classic 'coated aorta' or intracardiac infiltrative disease consistent with ECD.
Consult with a hematopathologist specializing in histiocytic disorders to review the biopsy slides for subtle evidence of emperipolesis or histiocytic morphology.
Evaluate for symptomatic management of bone pain and initiate discussion regarding targeted therapy (e.g., BRAF inhibitors if mutation positive or MEK inhibitors) vs. long-term immunomodulation.
